sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global equities advanced as Middle East de-escalation hopes and falling oil lifted risk appetite, with leadership broadening beyond mega-cap tech. MSCI World approx. +1.5% (approx., verify before merging), MSCI Developed Markets approx. +1.5% (approx., verify before merging) and MSCI Emerging Markets approx. +1% (approx., verify before merging). The S&P 500 added roughly +0.8% and small-caps surged, the Russell 2000 +3.9%, while the MAG7 mega-cap tech cohort lagged, broadly flat to lower amid a midweek AI-stock sell-off (approx., verify before merging). Europe outperformed: Euro Stoxx 50 +2.1%, DAX +3.9%, FTSE 100 +2.7%. In Asia, the Nikkei 225 rose approx. +2% (approx., verify before merging), Hang Seng approx. +1.5% (approx., verify before merging) and China's CSI 300 was little changed (approx., verify before merging).

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content: US indices closed a volatile week higher as cautious optimism over a possible US-Iran agreement, declining oil prices and a broadening of leadership beyond large-cap technology offset mixed inflation data and a midweek sell-off in AI-related names. Small-caps led decisively, the Russell 2000 climbing 3.9%, while the S&P 500, Dow and Nasdaq each added more than 0.65%, with value outpacing growth. May CPI rose 4.2% y/y, the highest since April 2023, but core prices increased just 0.2%, below expectations and reinforcing the view that energy, not underlying demand, is driving the inflation impulse. Attention turned to the following week's FOMC meeting, new Chair Kevin Warsh's debut, with markets positioned for rates to stay on hold.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content: European equities outperformed, propelled by hopes of Middle East de-escalation and easing oil prices. Germany's DAX surged 3.9%, the UK's FTSE 100 climbed 2.7%, France's CAC 40 rose 2.1% and Italy's FTSE MIB added 0.8%, lifting the Euro Stoxx 50 around 2.1% on the week. The ECB delivered a widely expected 25bp hike to 2.25%, its first increase since September 2023, citing broadening price pressures from the recent energy shock; Governing Council member Wunsch signalled a further move could come as soon as next month should inflation broaden. Even so, markets pared expectations for additional tightening as crude retreated, and the hawkish-leaning decision offered only limited support to risk sentiment, which was instead driven by the improving geopolitical backdrop and softer oil.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content: APAC markets were mixed-to-higher, lagging the stronger gains in the US and Europe. Japanese equities advanced, with the Nikkei 225 supported by a steadier yen and improving global risk sentiment as oil prices eased, though gains were tempered by a midweek pullback in technology shares following Wall Street's AI-related sell-off. Chinese stocks were more subdued: the CSI 300 ended little changed as soft domestic demand and the absence of fresh stimulus capped enthusiasm, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng edged higher in line with the regional risk-on tone. Across Asia ex-Japan, sentiment tracked the de-escalation in the Middle East and relief from falling energy costs, which matter particularly for the region's net energy importers, even as the global technology cycle remained a source of intermittent volatility.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content: The US dollar softened modestly as falling oil eroded the greenback's energy-exporter premium and improving risk appetite drew flows into cyclical currencies. Even so, the euro and sterling struggled to make decisive headway: EUR/USD failed to reclaim the 1.1550 area after slipping below 1.16 the prior week on firm US data, drawing only limited support from a hawkish-leaning ECB, while GBP could not convincingly recapture 1.34. The yen firmed somewhat as US yields edged lower and crude retreated, easing imported-inflation pressures. With conviction thin ahead of a heavy central-bank calendar, the Fed plus a UK by-election wildcard for sterling looming the following week, major pairs stayed largely rangebound, leaving rate differentials and the oil trajectory as the dominant near-term drivers.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content: Commodities extended a broad retreat as the geopolitical risk premium unwound on hopes of a US-Iran de-escalation. The Bloomberg Commodity Index fell around 4.5% on the week, a third consecutive week of aggressive selling by managed money, with liquidation concentrated in Brent crude, gold, soybeans and corn. Brent led the decline as traders unwound positions built during the earlier Middle East crisis, cutting the net long to a five-month low near 209k contracts, less than half the prior peak. Gold extended its slide, closing below its 200-day moving average for the first time since 2023 as a firmer risk tone, steadier real yields and reduced haven demand weighed. The aggressive deleveraging has left positioning notably leaner, reducing forced-liquidation risk and leaving the complex better placed to respond to fresh fundamental signals.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content: Government bonds rallied modestly as easing oil prices and a softer core inflation print tempered near-term rate concerns. The 10-year UST yield slipped around 5bp to roughly 4.48%, with declines led by the front end, as the May core CPI undershoot and late-week US-Iran de-escalation hopes lifted sentiment; the Bloomberg US Aggregate gained about 0.5%, reversing the prior week's losses. In Europe, 10-year Bund yields fell toward 2.9%, their lowest since mid-March, as traders pared back further ECB-hike expectations even after the central bank's 25bp increase. Credit held a firm tone, with IG and HY spreads broadly stable amid the risk-on backdrop. Investors largely looked past the data to the following week's FOMC decision, Chair Warsh's debut, where rates were widely expected to remain on hold.
